intro:
Pontus and Bithynia were two regions of northern Asia Minor along the southern shore of the Black Sea, joined by Rome into a single province. Bithynia lay to the west, with cities such as Nicomedia and Nicaea; Pontus stretched eastward along the coast, with ports like Sinope and Amisus. Mountainous and well-watered, the territory looked north to the sea and south across the Anatolian plateau, a meeting-ground of Greek colonists and native peoples.

The province has a striking place in the gospel’s spread. On his second journey Paul and his companions “attempted to go into Bithynia, but the Spirit of Jesus did not allow them” (Acts 16:7), turning them instead toward Troas, the vision of the man of Macedonia, and the carrying of the gospel into Europe — so the closed door of Bithynia became, in God’s providence, the opening of the West. A man of Pontus, Aquila, had come from there to Italy and then to Corinth, where he and Priscilla became Paul’s fellow workers (Acts 18:2), and visitors from Pontus had stood among the nations at Pentecost (Acts 2:9).

Though Paul was kept from Bithynia, the gospel reached it by other hands. Peter addressed his first epistle to “the elect exiles of the dispersion in Pontus, Galatia, Cappadocia, Asia, and Bithynia” (1 Peter 1:1), proof of churches already scattered across the province. Within two generations the Christians of Bithynia were so numerous that the governor wrote to the emperor for counsel on how to deal with them — a testimony that the faith took deep root in the very region once closed to the apostle.

Pontus and Bithynia thus bear witness to the sovereign wisdom of the Spirit, who both forbids and opens, weaving even a shut door into the wider purpose of Christ. The same Lord who redirected Paul to Europe was already gathering for himself a people on the Black Sea coast, “elect exiles” kept by his power through faith for a salvation ready to be revealed (1 Peter 1:5). So the province stands as a sign that no corner of the world lies beyond the gospel’s reach, and that the Shepherd and Overseer of souls seeks and keeps his own in every land (1 Peter 2:25).

link_tables.title: Places

cells: Western region Paul was forbidden by the Spirit to enter (Acts 16:7)
Eastern Black Sea region, home of Aquila and of dispersed believers (Acts 18:2)
Where Paul turned aside from Bithynia and saw the Macedonian vision (Acts 16:8–9)

link_tables.note: Click any name to open its article. Markers for these places appear on the map above.

cells: Wrote his first epistle to the believers of Pontus and Bithynia (1 Pet 1:1)
A Jew born in Pontus, fellow worker with Paul (Acts 18:2)
Forbidden by the Spirit of Jesus to enter Bithynia (Acts 16:7)
With Paul when the Spirit redirected them from Bithynia (Acts 16:6–7)
Newly joined to Paul’s band at the turning toward Troas (Acts 16:3)
